nodes.title: Define an event as a subset of the sample space
nodes.brief: Recognise that any subset of the sample space of a random experiment is an event, and translate a described happening into the matching subset of S.
nodes.quote: Any subset E of a sample space S is called *an event*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What a sample space is.
A random experiment is one whose outcome cannot be predicted in advance but whose set of all possible outcomes is known. This set of all outcomes is the sample space S; it plays the role of a universal set for the experiment. For tossing a coin twice, S = {HH, HT, TH, TT}.

PART 2 — Defining an event as a subset of S.
Any subset E of S is called an event. A described happening corresponds to exactly the subset of S whose elements are the outcomes that realise it. 'Exactly one head' corresponds to E = {HT, TH} inside S = {HH, HT, TH, TT}.

PART 3 — Translating a description into its subset.
To build an event: list every outcome in S, test each against the description, and collect those that pass. For 'at least one tail' on two coin tosses: test HH (no tail — fail), HT (one tail — pass), TH (one tail — pass), TT (two tails — pass), giving E = {HT, TH, TT}.
keyMoves: Write the sample space S of the experiment as the set of all outcomes
Translate a described happening into the subset of S whose outcomes realise it
State that this subset is the event
misconceptions: Confusing the event (a subset) with a single outcome (a sample point)
Listing outcomes that do not actually satisfy the described condition

nodes.title: Decide whether an event has occurred from the outcome
nodes.brief: Use the rule that an event E occurs exactly when the actual outcome of the experiment belongs to E, and does not occur when the outcome lies outside E.
nodes.quote: the event E of a sample space S is said to have occurred if the outcome  $\omega$  of the experiment is such that  $\omega \in E$ .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the actual outcome of the experiment.
When an experiment is performed it yields exactly one actual outcome, called omega. For example, rolling a die and seeing '3' gives omega = 3; tossing two coins and seeing heads then tails gives omega = HT.

PART 2 — Test whether omega belongs to the event subset E.
The event E has occurred if and only if the actual outcome lies inside E: omega in E means E occurred; omega not in E means E did not occur. For E = 'a number less than 4 appears' = {1, 2, 3} on a die: omega = 2 is in E so E occurred, omega = 5 is not in E so E did not occur.

PART 3 — Apply the membership test to decide occurrence.
This test is the bridge between the abstract subset and what physically happened. If E = {HT, TH} ('exactly one head in two coin tosses') and the outcome is TH, then TH in E so E has occurred. If the outcome is HH, then HH not in E so E has not occurred.
keyMoves: Identify the actual outcome omega produced by the experiment
Check whether omega belongs to the event subset E
Conclude E occurred if omega in E, and did not occur if omega not in E
misconceptions: Thinking an event occurs only if every outcome in E happens, rather than the single observed outcome lying in E
Judging occurrence from the description in words instead of testing membership in the subset

nodes.title: Identify impossible and sure events
nodes.brief: Recognise the empty set as the impossible event and the whole sample space as the sure event, and classify a described event as one of these when appropriate.
nodes.quote: is called an *impossible event* and S, i.e., the whole sample space is called the *sure event*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — List the subset for the described event.
Start with S and list every outcome that satisfies the description. If no outcome satisfies it, the subset is phi (empty); if every outcome satisfies it, the subset is S itself. These two extremes are the only cases that force you into the impossible or sure categories.

PART 2 — Identify the impossible event as phi.
The empty set phi is the impossible event: it can never occur because it has no outcomes. For a standard die (S = {1,2,3,4,5,6}), 'a multiple of 7 appears' corresponds to phi — no face shows a multiple of 7 — so this event is impossible.

PART 3 — Identify the sure event as S.
The whole sample space S is the sure event: it is certain to occur because every possible outcome lies in S. 'The number turns up is odd or even' (every positive integer is one or the other) is S for a die, and is guaranteed.
keyMoves: List the subset associated with the described event
If no outcome satisfies it, identify it as the impossible event phi
If every outcome satisfies it, identify it as the sure event S
misconceptions: Treating an unlikely-but-possible event as impossible
Forgetting that the sure event is the entire sample space, not just a large subset

nodes.title: Identify a simple (elementary) event
nodes.brief: Recognise an event with exactly one sample point as a simple event, and count that a space of n outcomes has exactly n simple events.
nodes.quote: If an event E has only one sample point of a sample space, it is called a *simple* (or *elementary*) *event*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Count the sample points in the event.
Write the event E as a subset of S and count how many elements it contains. The count (one, or more than one) determines the type: exactly one sample point makes a simple event, more than one makes a compound event.

PART 2 — Classify an event as simple when it has exactly one sample point.
A simple (elementary) event is a singleton subset of S. For S = {HH, HT, TH, TT} from two coin tosses, {HH} is a simple event because it contains exactly one sample point. The full description 'both tosses gave heads' picks out that single outcome.

PART 3 — Count the total number of simple events in S.
A sample space with n distinct elements has exactly n simple events, one for each outcome. For two coin tosses n = 4, so there are four simple events: {HH}, {HT}, {TH}, {TT}. Every event is a union of some of these building blocks.
keyMoves: Count the sample points in the event
Classify it as simple precisely when it has exactly one sample point
Note a space of n outcomes has exactly n simple events
misconceptions: Thinking a simple event is just any small or likely event rather than a singleton
Confusing the number of simple events with the number of events (subsets) of S

nodes.title: Identify a compound event
nodes.brief: Recognise an event with more than one sample point as a compound event, and write the subset of S it corresponds to.
nodes.quote: If an event has more than one sample point, it is called a *Compound event*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Write out the subset of S for the described event.
List every outcome in S and keep those that satisfy the description. For three coin tosses (S has 8 outcomes), 'exactly one head appeared' passes HTT, THT, TTH and rejects the other five, giving E = {HTT, THT, TTH}.

PART 2 — Count its sample points and classify as compound.
Count the elements in E. If there are more than one, E is a compound event. Here |E| = 3 > 1, so E is compound. A compound event is a union of two or more simple events: E = {HTT} union {THT} union {TTH}.

PART 3 — Recognise that compound events cover most practical questions.
Most events of practical interest — 'at least one head', 'sum exceeds 8 on two dice', 'committee has at least one woman' — are compound. Each is handled by listing its outcomes and summing their probabilities.
keyMoves: Write out the subset of S for the described event
Count its sample points
Classify it as compound when it has more than one sample point
misconceptions: Calling a singleton event compound
Failing to list every outcome that satisfies the description, undercounting the sample points

nodes.title: Form the complementary event 'not A'
nodes.brief: Construct the complement A' of an event A as the set of outcomes of S not in A, i.e. A' = S - A, the event 'not A'.
nodes.quote: A' = \{\omega : \omega \in S \text{ and } \omega \notin A\} = S - A.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Take S and remove the outcomes in A.
The complement of event A, written A' (or 'not A'), consists of every outcome in the sample space S that is not in A. Formally A' = {omega : omega in S and omega not in A} = S - A. Removing A from S leaves exactly the outcomes where A did not occur.

PART 2 — Write A' = S - A and verify no outcome of A appears.
For three coin tosses with S = {HHH, HHT, HTH, THH, HTT, THT, TTH, TTT} and A = {HTH, HHT, THH} ('only one tail appears'), remove the three outcomes of A from S to get A' = {HHH, HTT, THT, TTH, TTT}. None of A's elements appear in A', confirming no overlap.

PART 3 — Read A' as the event 'not A'.
A' and A together cover the whole S with no overlap: A union A' = S and A intersect A' = phi. Whenever the experiment result is in A', the event 'not A' has occurred. This partition is the foundation of the complement rule P(not A) = 1 - P(A).
keyMoves: Take the whole sample space S and remove the outcomes that lie in A
Write A' = S - A = {omega in S : omega not in A}
Read A' as the event 'not A'
misconceptions: Including some outcomes of A in the complement
Forgetting that the complement is taken relative to the full sample space S

nodes.title: Form the event 'A or B' as the union
nodes.brief: Build the event 'A or B' as the union A union B, containing every outcome in A, in B, or in both.
nodes.quote: When the sets A and B are two events associated with a sample space, then 'A  $\cup$  B' is the event 'either A or B or both'.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Collect every outcome in A or in B (or both) into one set.
The event 'A or B' contains every outcome that satisfies at least one of the two conditions. Start with all elements of A, then add any elements of B that are not already listed. Duplicates are not repeated: outcomes common to both appear only once in the union.

PART 2 — Write the result as A union B.
Formally A union B = {omega : omega in A or omega in B}. For a die with A = {2, 3, 5} (prime) and B = {1, 3, 5} (odd), the union collects 2, 3, 5 from A then adds 1 from B (3 and 5 already present), giving A union B = {1, 2, 3, 5}.

PART 3 — Interpret A union B as 'either A or B or both'.
The word 'or' in probability is always inclusive: A union B occurs whenever the outcome is in A, in B, or in both simultaneously. The outcome 3 (prime and odd) puts the experiment in both A and B; the outcome 2 (prime but not odd) puts it only in A — both lie in A union B.
keyMoves: Collect every outcome that is in A or in B (or both) into one set
Write the result as A union B
Interpret A union B as the event 'either A or B or both'
misconceptions: Treating 'or' as exclusive and dropping the outcomes common to A and B
Listing an outcome that is in neither A nor B

nodes.title: Form the event 'A and B' as the intersection
nodes.brief: Build the event 'A and B' as the intersection A intersect B, containing exactly the outcomes common to both events.
nodes.quote: If A and B are two events, then the set  $A \cap B$  denotes the event 'A and B'.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Find the outcomes that lie in A and also in B.
The event 'A and B' requires both conditions to be satisfied simultaneously. Go through each element of A and ask: is it also in B? Only those that pass both tests belong to A intersect B.

PART 2 — Collect just those common outcomes into one set.
Formally A intersect B = {omega : omega in A and omega in B}. For two throws of a die with A = 'first throw is six' = {(6,1),(6,2),(6,3),(6,4),(6,5),(6,6)} and B = 'sum is at least 11' = {(5,6),(6,5),(6,6)}, the common outcomes (those in both lists) are (6,5) and (6,6), so A intersect B = {(6,5), (6,6)}.

PART 3 — Write A intersect B as the event 'A and B'.
A intersect B occurs exactly when the outcome satisfies both A and B simultaneously. It is the correction term subtracted in the addition rule: when you add P(A) + P(B) you count the outcomes in A intersect B twice, so you must subtract P(A intersect B) once.
keyMoves: Find the outcomes that lie in A and also in B
Collect just those common outcomes into one set
Write the result as A intersect B, the event 'A and B'
misconceptions: Including outcomes that lie in only one of A or B
Confusing the intersection (and) with the union (or)

nodes.title: Form the event 'A but not B' as the difference
nodes.brief: Build the event 'A but not B' as the set difference A - B, equal to A intersect B', the outcomes in A that are excluded from B.
nodes.quote: the set A–B may denote the event 'A but not B'.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Start from the outcomes in A.
The event 'A but not B' asks: which outcomes are in A, but not in B? Begin by listing A. For a die with A = {2, 3, 5} (prime) and B = {1, 3, 5} (odd), start with A = {2, 3, 5}.

PART 2 — Remove any outcomes that also lie in B.
Cross out every element of A that appears in B. Here 3 and 5 appear in both A and B, so remove them. What remains is A - B = {2}. The result is the event 'prime but not odd' — which on a die means only the number 2.

PART 3 — Write A - B = A intersect B', the event 'A but not B'.
Equivalently, A - B = A intersect B' (outcomes in A that lie in the complement of B). This identity lets you compute the difference using the complement already defined: B' = {2, 4, 6}, and A intersect B' = {2, 3, 5} intersect {2, 4, 6} = {2}, confirming the result. The difference event is the tool for 'exactly one of A, B' problems.
keyMoves: Start from the outcomes in A
Remove any of them that also lie in B
Write the result as A - B = A intersect B', the event 'A but not B'
misconceptions: Computing B - A instead of A - B (the difference is not symmetric)
Forgetting that A - B keeps only outcomes that are in A

nodes.title: Test whether two events are mutually exclusive
nodes.brief: Decide that two events are mutually exclusive exactly when they cannot occur together, i.e. when their intersection is empty (disjoint sets).
nodes.quote: two events A and B are called *mutually exclusive* events if the occurrence of any one of them excludes the occurrence of the other event, i.e., if they can not occur simultaneously.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Write the subsets for both events.
List the outcomes in each event explicitly before testing mutual exclusivity. For a die, A = 'odd' = {1, 3, 5} and B = 'even' = {2, 4, 6}. Another pair: A = {1, 3, 5} and C = 'less than 4' = {1, 2, 3}.

PART 2 — Form their intersection A intersect B.
A and B are mutually exclusive exactly when A intersect B = phi (they are disjoint). For A = {1,3,5} and B = {2,4,6}: A intersect B = phi — no outcome is both odd and even — so they are mutually exclusive. For A = {1,3,5} and C = {1,2,3}: A intersect C = {1,3} is not empty, so A and C are not mutually exclusive (the outcome 3 satisfies both).

PART 3 — Declare mutual exclusivity only when A intersect B = phi.
Mutual exclusivity is the condition under which the simple addition rule P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) holds without an overlap correction. Always test the intersection explicitly — events that look different can still share an outcome.
keyMoves: Write the subsets for both events
Form their intersection A intersect B
Declare them mutually exclusive precisely when A intersect B = phi (disjoint)
misconceptions: Calling events mutually exclusive when they merely look different but actually share an outcome
Thinking mutually exclusive is the same as exhaustive

nodes.title: Test whether events are exhaustive (and mutually exclusive)
nodes.brief: Decide that events are exhaustive when their union is the whole sample space, and mutually exclusive and exhaustive when they are also pairwise disjoint.
nodes.quote: events  $E_1, E_2, ..., E_n$  are said to be exhaustive if atleast one of them necessarily occurs whenever the experiment is performed.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Form the union of all the events.
To test exhaustiveness, take the union E1 union E2 union ... union En. Collect every outcome that appears in at least one of the events. For a die with A = {1,2,3}, B = {3,4}, C = {5,6}: union = {1,2,3,4,5,6} = S.

PART 2 — Call them exhaustive when that union equals S.
Events are exhaustive if at least one of them must occur whenever the experiment is performed — equivalently their union covers all of S. The die example above is exhaustive. Note: exhaustive does NOT require disjointness; A and B above share the outcome 3.

PART 3 — Call them mutually exclusive and exhaustive when they also partition S.
If the events are additionally pairwise disjoint (Ei intersect Ej = phi for i not equal to j) as well as exhaustive, they partition S into non-overlapping pieces. Three-coin toss: A = {TTT}, B = {HTT,THT,TTH}, C = {HHT,HTH,THH,HHH} — their union is all 8 outcomes and no two share an outcome, so they are mutually exclusive and exhaustive.
keyMoves: Form the union of all the events
Call them exhaustive when that union equals the sample space S
Call them mutually exclusive and exhaustive when they are also pairwise disjoint (Ei intersect Ej = phi)
misconceptions: Thinking exhaustive requires the events to be disjoint (it only requires their union to be S)
Forgetting to check the pairwise-disjoint condition before claiming mutually exclusive and exhaustive

nodes.title: State the axioms of probability
nodes.brief: State the axiomatic definition of probability as a function on the power set of S into [0,1] satisfying non-negativity, P(S)=1, and additivity for mutually exclusive events.
nodes.quote: The probability P is a real valued function whose domain is the power set of S and range is the interval [0,1]
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — State axiom (i): non-negativity P(E) >= 0 for every event E.
Probability P is a real-valued function whose domain is the power set of S (the set of all events — every subset of S) and whose range is [0,1]. The first axiom says P(E) >= 0 for every event E: no event can have a negative probability. Any candidate assignment that gives a negative value to an outcome is immediately invalid.

PART 2 — State axiom (ii): P(S) = 1, the certain event has probability 1.
The second axiom fixes the scale: the sure event S has probability exactly 1. For a finite sample space S = {omega_1, ..., omega_n} this forces P(omega_1) + P(omega_2) + ... + P(omega_n) = 1; every probability assignment must sum to 1 across all outcomes.

PART 3 — State axiom (iii): additivity for mutually exclusive events, and deduce P(phi) = 0.
The third axiom says: if E and F are mutually exclusive (E intersect F = phi), then P(E union F) = P(E) + P(F). Setting F = phi gives P(E union phi) = P(E) + P(phi), i.e. P(E) = P(E) + P(phi), so P(phi) = 0 — the impossible event has probability 0.
keyMoves: State axiom (i): P(E) >= 0 for every event E
State axiom (ii): P(S) = 1
State axiom (iii): mutually exclusive E, F give P(E union F) = P(E) + P(F), and deduce P(phi) = 0
misconceptions: Allowing a probability greater than 1 or a negative probability
Applying the additivity axiom to events that are not mutually exclusive

nodes.title: Compute P(A) by summing the probabilities of its outcomes
nodes.brief: Find the probability of any event by adding the assigned probabilities of the sample points it contains: P(A) = sum of P(omega_i) over omega_i in A.
nodes.quote: For any event A,  $P(A) = \sum P(\omega_i), \omega_i \in A$ .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — List the sample points that belong to the event A.
Given a sample space S with assigned probabilities on each outcome, identify exactly which outcomes lie inside event A. Do not include outcomes outside A; do not miss any inside. For three consecutive pens (B=bad, G=good) with A = 'exactly one bad pen', A = {BGG, GBG, GGB}.

PART 2 — Add their assigned probabilities P(omega_i).
P(A) is the sum of P(omega_i) for all omega_i in A. If each outcome has probability 1/8 (eight equally likely sequences), then P(A) = 1/8 + 1/8 + 1/8 = 3/8. The same rule works for unequal assignments: P(HH) = 1/4, P(HT) = 1/7, P(TH) = 2/7, P(TT) = 9/28 gives P({HH,TT}) = 1/4 + 9/28 = 7/28 + 9/28 = 16/28 = 4/7.

PART 3 — Report the sum as P(A), valid even when outcomes are not equally likely.
This formula P(A) = sum P(omega_i) follows directly from the additivity axiom applied repeatedly. It does NOT require equal probabilities — the only requirement is that the individual P(omega_i) form a valid assignment (non-negative, summing to 1 over all of S).
keyMoves: List the sample points that belong to the event A
Add their assigned probabilities P(omega_i)
Report the sum as P(A), valid even when outcomes are not equally likely
misconceptions: Assuming every outcome has probability 1/n even when the assignment is unequal
Summing probabilities of outcomes that are not in A

nodes.title: Compute probability for equally likely outcomes as n(A)/n(S)
nodes.brief: When all outcomes are equally likely, compute the probability of an event as the ratio of favourable outcomes to total outcomes, P(A) = n(A)/n(S).
nodes.quote: Probability of an event  $P(A) = \frac{n(A)}{n(S)}$ , where n(A) = number of elements in
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Confirm the outcomes are equally likely, so each has probability 1/n.
If a sample space has n outcomes and each is equally likely, then every outcome must have the same probability p. Since all n probabilities sum to 1, we get np = 1, so p = 1/n. This is the key assumption: do NOT use this formula unless equally likely outcomes are justified (fair coin, fair die, random draw from identical objects).

PART 2 — Count n(A), the favourable outcomes, and n(S), the total outcomes.
For the event A, count how many of the n outcomes satisfy the condition: call this m = n(A). For a deck of 52 cards equally likely, diamonds number 13 so n(A) = 13 and n(S) = 52. Counting often requires combinations from maths-ch06 for unordered selections.

PART 3 — Compute P(A) = n(A)/n(S).
P(A) = m/n = n(A)/n(S). Probability of a diamond: 13/52 = 1/4. Probability of a black card: 26/52 = 1/2. Probability of an ace: 4/52 = 1/13. The formula combines with combinations for larger problems: 4 kings from 52 gives P(4 kings in 7-card hand) = C(4,4)*C(48,3)/C(52,7).
keyMoves: Confirm the outcomes are equally likely, so each has probability 1/n
Count n(A), the favourable outcomes, and n(S), the total outcomes
Compute P(A) = n(A)/n(S)
misconceptions: Using n(A)/n(S) when the outcomes are not equally likely
Miscounting n(A) or n(S), e.g. by double counting or missing arrangements

nodes.title: Apply the addition theorem P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)
nodes.brief: Compute the probability of a union with the addition theorem, subtracting the overlap, and reduce it to P(A) + P(B) when the events are mutually exclusive.
nodes.quote: Thus we observe that,  $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute P(A), P(B), and the overlap P(A intersect B).
Before applying the addition theorem, find all three quantities. If A = 'Anil qualifies' with P(A) = 0.05, B = 'Ashima qualifies' with P(B) = 0.10, and P(A intersect B) = 0.02 (both qualify), the overlap has been counted once in P(A) and once again in P(B).

PART 2 — Apply P(A union B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A intersect B).
Subtract the overlap exactly once to correct the double-count: P(A union B) = 0.05 + 0.10 - 0.02 = 0.13. This is the probability that at least one of them qualifies. The formula holds for any two events regardless of whether they share outcomes.

PART 3 — Drop the overlap term when A and B are mutually exclusive.
If A intersect B = phi then P(A intersect B) = 0, and the formula reduces to P(A union B) = P(A) + P(B), which is exactly axiom (iii). Never subtract a zero overlap as if it were non-zero, and never forget to subtract a real overlap.
keyMoves: Compute P(A), P(B) and the overlap P(A intersect B)
Apply P(A union B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A intersect B)
Drop the overlap term to P(A) + P(B) when A and B are mutually exclusive
misconceptions: Adding P(A) + P(B) without subtracting the overlap when the events are not mutually exclusive
Subtracting an overlap for mutually exclusive events (where it is zero) or getting its sign wrong

nodes.title: Apply the complement rule P(not A) = 1 - P(A)
nodes.brief: Use the complement rule to find the probability of 'not A' from P(A), exploiting that A and A' are mutually exclusive and exhaustive.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Recognise A and A' are mutually exclusive and exhaustive, so P(A) + P(A') = 1.
Every event A and its complement A' partition S: A intersect A' = phi (mutually exclusive) and A union A' = S (exhaustive). By the axioms, P(A) + P(A') = P(S) = 1. This holds for every event A without exception.

PART 2 — Compute P(not A) = 1 - P(A).
Rearranging gives P(A') = P(not A) = 1 - P(A). For a card drawn from 10 equally likely cards with A = {2,4,6,8}: P(A) = 4/10 = 2/5, so P(not A) = 1 - 2/5 = 3/5. For an ace: P(ace) = 4/52 = 1/13, so P(not ace) = 12/13.

PART 3 — Use 1 - P(none) for 'at least one' problems.
The complement rule is the fastest route when the 'at least one' event is hard to count directly but its complement 'none' is easy. P(at least one head in 4 tosses) = 1 - P(no heads) = 1 - (1/2)^4 = 1 - 1/16 = 15/16. Always check: is the complement simpler than the event itself?
keyMoves: Recognise A and A' are mutually exclusive and exhaustive, so P(A) + P(A') = 1
Compute P(not A) = 1 - P(A)
Use it to turn an awkward event into 1 minus its complement (e.g. 'at least one' = 1 - P(none))
misconceptions: Writing P(not A) = P(A) - 1 or otherwise getting the subtraction backwards
Applying the complement rule to two events that are not actually complementary

nodes.title: Solve probability problems using events and the axiomatic rules
nodes.brief: Combine event algebra with the axiomatic rules — equally likely counting, the addition theorem and the complement rule — to compute the probability of any event in a random experiment.
nodes.quote: In this Chapter, we studied about the axiomatic approach of probability.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Translate the described event into set operations on S.
Every probability problem starts with building the right event. Read the description and express it using complement (A'), union (A union B), intersection (A intersect B), or difference (A - B). For 'both Anil and Ashima do not qualify' write E' intersect F' = (E union F)'; for 'only one qualifies' write (E intersect F') union (E' intersect F). Getting the event right is half the problem.

PART 2 — Choose the right rule: equally likely counting, addition theorem, or complement rule.
For equally likely experiments: P(A) = n(A)/n(S) (use combinations to count). For unions: P(A union B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A intersect B), reduced to P(A) + P(B) when mutually exclusive. For complements: P(A') = 1 - P(A); use this for 'at least one' via 1 - P(none). One problem may need multiple rules applied in sequence.

PART 3 — Combine the rules to get the final probability.
Example: 'at least one of A, B qualifies' = P(E union F) = P(E) + P(F) - P(E intersect F) = 0.05 + 0.10 - 0.02 = 0.13. 'Only one qualifies' = P(E) - P(E intersect F) + P(F) - P(E intersect F) = 0.05 - 0.02 + 0.10 - 0.02 = 0.11. For committees, relay races, and card problems, count n(A) and n(S) with combinations, then form the ratio.
keyMoves: Translate the described event into set operations on the sample space
Choose the right rule: equally likely counting n(A)/n(S), addition theorem, or complement rule
Combine them (e.g. 1 - P(none), or P(A or B) with overlap subtracted) to get the final probability
misconceptions: Reaching for a counting ratio n(A)/n(S) when outcomes are not equally likely
Adding probabilities of overlapping events without subtracting the intersection
